title: Multi-cycle iterative divider to break the 32-bit DIV/REM critical path
motivation:
The baseline ALU implements DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU as combinational
$signed/unsigned `/` and `%` operators inside the EX-stage ALU
(rtl/alu.sv). A 32-bit divide synthesizes to a long restoring or
non-restoring tree of subtract/compare cells; on FPGA fabric (no
hard divider IP on the Tang Nano 20K's GW2AR-LV) this cone is the
classic Fmax killer. Yosys/nextpnr-himbaechel will route it through
general LUT4s and the 9k-LUT design's worst negative slack will
almost certainly land in this cone.

CoreMark's hot loops (matrix multiply-accumulate, list traversal,
state-machine character classifier) emit *zero* DIV/REM
instructions in the inner kernels — the only divides occur in the
one-time CRC seed setup and the iter/sec calculation that runs
after `stop_time`. So multi-cycling the divider trades essentially
zero IPC for whatever Fmax headroom the combinational divider was
costing. On equivalent RV32 cores published Fmax for Gowin GW2A
fabric typically jumps 25–40% when the iterative divider replaces
the `/` operator.

Multiplier is left alone for this hypothesis. The 32x32 multiply on
the Tang Nano 20K maps to chained 18x18 DSP slices, which are
meaningfully shorter combinationally than the divide cone. Touching
one variable at a time keeps the experiment attribution clean — if
Fmax doesn't move, we know the divider wasn't the bottleneck and a
follow-up hypothesis can pipeline the multiplier next.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU operators in
rtl/alu.sv with a 33-cycle non-restoring iterative divider, gated
by a start/busy/done handshake. The ALU exposes a `div_busy` flag;
the EX stage stalls the pipeline (holds IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM and
drops a bubble into MEM/WB) for the duration of the divide. When
`done` asserts, the EX/MEM register captures the iterative result
and the pipeline resumes.

Implementation outline:
  - New module rtl/divu.sv: a 33-state FSM operating on an unsigned
    33-bit partial-remainder / 32-bit quotient pair. Sign handling
    is done at the ALU boundary (compute sign of inputs, run unsigned
    divide, fix up sign of quotient and remainder per RV32M rules).
    Edge cases (b == 0; INT_MIN / -1) are short-circuited to a
    single-cycle result and don't enter the FSM.
  - rtl/alu.sv switches DIV/REM ops to consume the iterative divider's
    result. MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU stay combinational (separate
    hypothesis if needed).
  - rtl/alu.sv exposes new ports: `start_div`, `div_busy`, `div_done`.
  - rtl/ex_stage.sv: detect div/rem in `in.ctrl.alu_op`, raise
    `start_div` on the first cycle the op enters EX, stall EX/MEM
    register capture and forward `div_busy` to hazard_unit. ALTOPS
    branch in alu.sv stays combinational (riscv-formal speed).
  - rtl/hazard_unit.sv: new `div_busy` input ORed into stall_if /
    stall_id / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb on the same priority as
    dmem_stall (preserves load-use semantics; div_busy is mutually
    exclusive with dmem_stall because a div is in EX, not MEM).
  - test/test_alu.py: extend to drive the new handshake — issue
    DIV/REM, assert `div_busy` rises, wait for `div_done`, check the
    result word matches the existing 30+ vectors. Keep all current
    bit-exact RV32M semantics (div by 0 -> -1, INT_MIN/-1 -> INT_MIN,
    etc.).

RVFI invariants are preserved because the EX/MEM register only
advances when `div_done` is high; rvfi_order increments exactly
once per div retirement; rvfi_trap, rs1/rs2/rd routing all flow
through the same ctrl_t bundle. Formal `unique` and `liveness`
checks pass as long as the divide FSM has a proven termination
bound (33 cycles), which it does.

changes.description:
New module. 33-cycle non-restoring unsigned divider with
start/busy/done handshake. State: 6-bit counter, 33-bit
partial-remainder accumulator, 32-bit quotient shifter,
32-bit divisor latch. On `start`, latches operands and runs
33 iterations of (shift left, trial subtract, set quotient
bit). On the final iteration drives `done` for one cycle with
`quotient` and `remainder` outputs valid.

Split DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU off the combinational `case (op)`.
Instantiate divu and route signed/unsigned operand prep at the
ALU boundary (per RV32M sign-fixup rules). Add ports `start_div`
(input), `div_busy` (output), `div_done` (output). Combinational
ALU result mux selects the divider output for div/rem ops; all
other ops remain combinational and 0-cycle. ALTOPS ifdef branch
stays exactly as today (XOR-of-add/sub formulas, combinational)
so the per-iteration formal gate keeps its 20-step BMC budget.

Detect div/rem in id_ex_w.ctrl.alu_op. On the first cycle one
enters EX, raise alu.start_div for one cycle. Drive a new
output `div_busy` to top level (consumed by hazard_unit).
EX/MEM register captures only when !div_busy || div_done.
Branch / redirect logic unchanged because no div instruction
is ever a branch / jump.

Add input `div_busy`. Update stall logic so:
  stall_if      |= div_busy
  stall_id      |= div_busy
  stall_ex_mem  |= div_busy
  hold_mem_wb   |= div_busy
div_busy is mutually exclusive with dmem_stall (dmem_stall
requires a memory op in EX/MEM; div_busy means a div is in EX).
No change to load-use / redirect priority.

Wire ex_stage's `div_busy` output into hazard_unit. No top-level
RVFI changes (retirement boundary unchanged).

Update existing M-extension vectors to drive the new
start/busy/done handshake. Each DIV/REM test now toggles
`start_div` for one cycle, waits up to 40 cycles for
`div_done`, then checks the result. All bit-exact RV32M
semantics preserved (b==0 sentinels, INT_MIN/-1 overflow).
Add a new test that issues back-to-back divides to verify the
FSM resets cleanly between ops.

implementation_notes:
# hyp-20260427-002 — multi-cycle iterative divider

## What changed

- **`rtl/divu.sv` (new)** — 33-cycle restoring binary divider FSM. State
  machine: `IDLE → BUSY (32 iters) → DONE (1 cycle) → IDLE`. Operands
  are latched on the IDLE→BUSY transition, so the caller need only
  hold `dividend`/`divisor` valid on the cycle `start` is asserted.
  `quot_q` and `rem_q` retain their values across `IDLE`/`DONE`, so a
  consumer that sees `done` may capture the result on any subsequent
  cycle until the next divide overwrites the operand latches.

- **`rtl/alu.sv`** — added `clock`, `reset`, `start_div`, `div_busy`,
  `div_done` ports. Under non-ALTOPS, instantiates `divu` and applies
  RV32M sign correction at the boundary (absolute value pre-divide,
  two's-complement post-divide). Edge cases (`b == 0`; signed
  `INT_MIN / -1`) are detected combinationally, bypass the FSM, and
  pulse `div_done` in the same cycle `start_div` is asserted with
  `div_busy` held at 0 — so EX/MEM captures the result without a
  stall. Under ALTOPS, the FSM is not instantiated and `div_done` is
  hardwired to 1 (combinational behavior matches the prior ALU; the
  XOR-of-add/sub formulas still drive `out` via the existing case arms).

- **`rtl/ex_stage.sv`** — drives the divider handshake. Two sticky
  flops (`div_started_q`, `div_completed_q`) break the
  `start_div ↔ alu.div_busy` combinational loop and remember a `done`
  observed during a concurrent dmem stall, so the divide is never
  re-triggered. Adds `div_busy` output to top-level. EX/MEM register
  capture is gated on `!stall && !div_pipeline_stall`.

- **`rtl/hazard_unit.sv`** — added `div_busy` input; ORed into
  `stall_if`, `stall_id`, `stall_ex_mem`, `hold_mem_wb`. `flush_id` is
  also gated `&& !div_busy` so the in-flight divide is held (not
  flushed) during the multi-cycle wait.

- **`rtl/core.sv`** — wires `ex_stage.div_busy` to
  `hazard_unit.div_busy` via a new `ex_div_busy` net.

- **`test/test_alu.py`** — extended every DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vector to
  drive the new handshake (`_check_div`): set operands + pulse
  `start_div`, poll `div_done` (with a 40-cycle timeout), capture
  `out`. Edge cases are exercised on the same code path — they exit
  the polling loop on the first sample because `div_done` is
  combinationally high. Added a new `back_to_back_divides` test that
  issues five consecutive iterative divides to verify the FSM resets
  cleanly between operations. All 30+ existing vectors retained.

- **`test/test_pipeline.py`** — added `divu.sv` to the runner's source
  list (in front of `alu.sv`) so the pipeline DUT compiles. Same
  ordering for the cosim build is automatic since
  `test/cosim/build.sh` globs `rtl/*.sv`.

## Verification

| Gate                                            | Result |
|-------------------------------------------------|--------|
| `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP`     | clean  |
| `verilator --lint-only ... -DRISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` | clean  |
| `pytest test/` (5 cocotb runners, 21 alu cases) | pass   |
| `make cosim` (selftest.elf + coremark.elf)      | pass   |

CoreMark cosim passing is the strong signal: the full pipeline retires
correctly under RVFI cross-check against the Python ISS for the entire
6 KB performance run, including any DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU emitted by the
CRC seed-setup and iter/sec computation.

## Deviations from the hypothesis plan

1. **`rem_q` is 32 bits, not 33.** The hypothesis described a "33-bit
   partial-remainder accumulator". Inside the FSM the partial
   remainder needs 33 bits during the shift-and-trial-subtract, but
   between iterations the remainder is strictly less than the divisor
   (which is < 2³²), so 32 register bits suffice. The 33rd bit is
   reconstructed combinationally each cycle. This was prompted by a
   Verilator UNUSEDSIGNAL warning on `rem_q[32]` and is logically
   equivalent.

2. **Two trackers in ex_stage, not one.** The hypothesis sketch
   implies a single "div in flight" flag; I needed two:
   `div_started_q` (so the start pulse self-extinguishes after one
   cycle) and `div_completed_q` (so a `div_done` observed during a
   dmem stall isn't lost, and the divide isn't re-triggered when the
   FSM transitions back to IDLE during the stall hold). Without
   `div_completed_q`, a dmem stall coinciding with the divide's
   `done` cycle would either drop the result or cause a re-trigger
   when divu wraps back through IDLE.

3. **Edge cases bypass the FSM.** The hypothesis allowed this
   (`Edge cases (b == 0; INT_MIN / -1) are short-circuited to a
   single-cycle result and don't enter the FSM`); my implementation
   does it by gating `divu_start` to 0 for edge cases and driving
   `alu.div_done = start_div && is_div_op` so EX/MEM captures the
   `edge_result` on the same cycle the divide enters EX.

4. **ALTOPS build hardwires `div_done = 1`.** Under
   `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, `div_done` is permanently asserted so
   ex_stage's `div_pipeline_stall` reduces to 0 — the formal gate
   sees the prior ALU's combinational latency for div/rem, keeping
   the per-iteration BMC depth budget intact. `div_busy` is 0 in
   this build.

## Concerns / things to watch

- **Mutual exclusion claim.** The hypothesis asserts `div_busy` and
  `dmem_stall` are mutually exclusive ("a div is in EX, not MEM").
  Strictly speaking they can coexist: a divide in EX while a
  preceding LOAD/STORE waits on the bus in EX/MEM. The
  implementation handles concurrent assertion correctly — `div_busy`
  and `dmem_stall` both gate `ex_capture` (logical OR), and
  `div_completed_q` survives the stall — but the comment in
  `hazard_unit.sv` is honest about the OR semantics rather than
  promising mutual exclusion.

- **Multiplier untouched.** The hypothesis explicitly chooses to
  leave MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU combinational. If the divider was *not*
  the Fmax bottleneck, the next hypothesis can pipeline the
  multiplier; this one trades zero CoreMark IPC (no inner-loop
  divides) for whatever divider headroom the iterative FSM unlocks.

- **`make formal-deep` not run here.** The fast formal build uses
  `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, which substitutes div/rem with XOR formulas
  — so the per-iteration formal gate proves bypassing/forwarding/trap
  discipline around div/rem, but does NOT prove the iterative
  divider's bit-exact RV32M arithmetic. That's covered by the cocotb
  vectors in `test_alu.py` and by cosim against
  `test/cosim/reference.py` (CoreMark exercises real divides during
  the CRC seed-setup phase). `make formal-deep` would prove
  arithmetic via SMT; it is run periodically per CLAUDE.md, not in
  this hypothesis.

title: Pipeline the 32x32 multiplier to break the M-extension MUL critical path
motivation:
After hyp-20260427-002 multi-cycled the divider, Fmax jumped from
127.03 -> 152.28 MHz (+19.9%) and the LUT4 count fell from 9563 ->
5369. That confirms the combinational divide cone owned the prior
worst negative slack. The next-most-likely Fmax limiter on this
design is the *combinational 32x32 multiplier* still living in
rtl/alu.sv: three full 64-bit products (mul_uu, mul_ss, mul_su)
generated in parallel inside the ALU's `always_comb`, all feeding
the result mux in the same cycle.

On the Tang Nano 20K (GW2AR-LV / GW2A-18C fabric) a 32x32 product
maps to a small array of chained 18x18 DSP cells. The DSP block
has an internal pipeline register (M_REG); when used in
zero-latency mode (the current code) the multiplication propagates
combinationally through the DSP cluster plus the soft logic that
reduces the partial products to a 64-bit result. Public Gowin
GW2A timing data and equivalent published RV32IM cores (PicoRV32
with hard MUL, VexRiscv "MulPlugin" with mulUnrolFactor=1) show
this cone running 7-9 ns combinationally, i.e. capping Fmax at
~110-140 MHz. We're already at 152 MHz, which means the timer is
almost certainly the new critical path now -- there is little
routing or ALU-mux delay left to absorb a multi-ns DSP cone.

CoreMark's hot kernels (matrix multiply-accumulate, list traverse,
state-machine classifier) DO emit MUL -- the matrix kernel is the
one place inner-loop multiplies happen. From the previous run's
4,491,817 cycles / 10 iters trace, a rough estimate (CoreMark v1.0
matrix dimension 25, 6 KB build) is ~3-5K MULs per iteration. At
one stall cycle per MUL that is ~30-50K extra cycles per iter, or
~7-11% IPC loss. A typical Gowin-fabric Fmax win from registering
out the DSP path is +12-20%. Net fitness is positive in expectation
and -- crucially -- the change is mechanically equivalent to the
divider hypothesis so we already have a proven handshake template
in ex_stage / hazard_unit.

Touching only the multiplier (not the branch comparator, not the
ALU shifter, not any RAM) keeps attribution clean: if Fmax doesn't
move, the multiplier wasn't the bottleneck and we move on to the
branch resolve cone or barrel shifter next iteration.
hypothesis:
Pipeline the 32x32 multiplier in rtl/alu.sv with a single internal
register stage, exposing the same start/busy/done handshake the
divider already uses. MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU become 1-cycle-latency
ops: when one enters EX, ex_stage pulses `start_mul`, the ALU
latches operands + selects the operation, the multiplier produces
the 64-bit product into a flop on the next clock edge, and on
cycle 1 `mul_done` pulses while `out` carries the selected high or
low half. ex_stage holds IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM and bubbles MEM/WB
for the one stall cycle, mirroring the divider gating.

Why one stall cycle (not zero, not two):
  - Zero stall cycles requires either (a) a 2-source multiplier
    slot in the pipeline (true superscalar / decoupled mul unit)
    or (b) a combinational multiplier, which is what we're trying
    to remove. Out of scope.
  - Two stall cycles (registered DSP M_REG + registered output
    reduction adder) would maximize Fmax but cost ~2x the IPC
    penalty. Not justified until/unless one stall doesn't free the
    timing.

Implementation outline:
  - New module rtl/mul.sv: synchronous 32x32 -> 64-bit signed and
    unsigned multiplier wrapping the three product variants
    (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, signed*unsigned). One
    pipeline register on the output path so Yosys can map the path
    through the GW2A DSP M_REG. Inputs `start`, `op_sel` (2 bits:
    uu / ss / su), `a`, `b`. Outputs `result_hi`, `result_lo`,
    `busy`, `done`.
  - rtl/alu.sv: split MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU off the combinational
    `case (op)` arm. Instantiate mul. Add ports `start_mul`
    (input), `mul_busy` (output), `mul_done` (output). For
    ALU_MUL select result_lo; for ALU_MULH/MULHU/MULHSU select
    result_hi. Edge-case detection (a==0 || b==0) MAY be folded
    into a 0-cycle bypass for common code (CoreMark's `crc16` and
    list-init paths), but the v1 implementation keeps every MUL
    one-cycle-latency to minimize moving parts.
  - rtl/alu.sv: ALTOPS branch (RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS) keeps the
    combinational XOR-of-add/sub formulas exactly as today, with
    mul_busy hardwired to 0 and mul_done hardwired to 1. The
    per-iteration formal gate sees zero-latency MUL, preserving
    its 20-step BMC depth budget (riscv-formal docs sec.7.6).
  - rtl/ex_stage.sv: extend the existing div handshake to a
    shared mul/div mechanism. New sticky flops `mul_started_q` /
    `mul_completed_q` (mirror of the div trackers) so the start
    pulse self-extinguishes and a `mul_done` observed during a
    concurrent dmem stall is not lost. New output `mul_busy` to
    top level; combine with `div_busy` into a single
    `m_ext_busy = div_busy || mul_busy` driven out of ex_stage to
    hazard_unit (or two parallel inputs -- whichever is cleaner).
    EX/MEM register capture is gated on
    `!stall && !div_pipeline_stall && !mul_pipeline_stall`.
  - rtl/hazard_unit.sv: add `mul_busy` input. ORed into stall_if,
    stall_id, stall_ex_mem, hold_mem_wb on the same priority as
    div_busy. flush_id is gated `&& !mul_busy` (mirror of the
    div_busy gate) so an in-flight multiply is held, not flushed.
    mul_busy and div_busy are mutually exclusive in single-issue
    (a single op cannot be both DIV and MUL), so the ORs simply
    union them.
  - rtl/core.sv: wire ex_stage's `mul_busy` to hazard_unit.
    Retirement boundary unchanged (rvfi_order still increments
    exactly once per MUL since EX/MEM only captures on the
    cycle mul_done is high).

RVFI / formal invariants preserved:
  - Invariant #1 (RVFI port set): top-level IO unchanged.
  - Invariant #2 (rvfi_trap = is_illegal): MUL ops were not
    illegal before and are not illegal now; ctrl_t routing
    unchanged.
  - Invariant #4 (rvfi_order monotonic +1): EX/MEM register only
    advances when mul_done is high, so MEM/WB increments order
    exactly once per MUL retirement -- identical to the proven
    divider mechanism.
  - Invariant #5 (liveness): the multiplier's pipeline register
    is unconditional (not gated by anything that could deadlock);
    mul_done asserts exactly one cycle after start_mul. Termination
    bound = 1 cycle, so riscv-formal liveness check passes for the
    same reason it passed for the 33-cycle divider.
  - Invariant #5b (M-ext bit-exact arithmetic): test_alu.py
    vectors retained, retargeted to drive the start/done handshake.
    cosim against test/cosim/reference.py exercises real CoreMark
    MULs end-to-end.
  - Invariants #3, #6-#10 are unrelated to the multiplier path
    and are not touched.

Expected fitness: Fmax +12-20% on Tang Nano 20K, IPC -7 to -11%
on CoreMark, net delta +5 to +13%. Confidence is medium because
the prior hypothesis already proved this exact handshake pattern
works through the full eval pipeline; the only unknown is whether
the multiplier really is the new critical path or if some other
cone (branch resolve, ALU shifter, regfile write port mux) takes
over once the DSP cluster is registered.

changes.description:
New module. Synchronous pipelined 32x32 -> 64-bit multiplier.
Inputs: clock, reset, start, op_sel (2-bit: 0=uu, 1=ss, 2=su),
a (32b), b (32b). Outputs: result_hi (32b), result_lo (32b),
busy (1b), done (1b).

Internal: latches a, b, op_sel on the cycle start is asserted.
One pipeline register on the 64-bit product so Yosys can map
the path through the GW2A DSP M_REG. busy = 1 from the cycle
start is asserted through the cycle done is asserted. done
pulses high one cycle after start. Latency is fixed at 1
cycle.

No reset behavior on data registers (operand latches and
product flop are don't-care under reset; only state needs
synchronous reset). Header comment documents purpose, latency
(1 cycle), and that no RVFI fields are driven here -- the
result feeds rd_wdata via alu -> EX/MEM/WB exactly as today.

Split MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU off the combinational `case (op)`
arm. Add ports: start_mul (input, 1b), mul_busy (output, 1b),
mul_done (output, 1b).

Under non-ALTOPS: instantiate `mul u_mul (...)`. Drive op_sel
from the ALU op (MUL/MULHU -> uu, MULH -> ss, MULHSU -> su).
Replace the three combinational `mul_uu = ... ; mul_ss = ...;
mul_su = ...` assigns and the corresponding case arms with a
single mux that picks result_lo (for MUL) or result_hi (for
MULH/MULHU/MULHSU) from u_mul.

Under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS: keep the combinational
XOR-of-add/sub formulas for MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU exactly as
today. mul_busy = 0, mul_done = 1 (always asserted) so
ex_stage's mul_pipeline_stall reduces to 0 in the formal
build. Identical philosophy to how div_done is hardwired
under ALTOPS in the current alu.sv.

Header comment latency line updated: "all non-mul/div ops:
combinational; MUL ops: 1 cycle; DIV/REM ops: 33 cycles
(real) or 1 cycle (edge case); ALTOPS build: combinational".

Mirror the existing div handshake for multiplies. Add:
  - is_mul_op = in.valid && (alu_op in {MUL, MULH, MULHU,
    MULHSU})
  - start_mul_w = is_mul_op && !mul_started_q && !mul_completed_q
  - mul_pipeline_stall = is_mul_op && !mul_completed_q && !alu_mul_done
Add sticky flops mul_started_q, mul_completed_q with the
same reset / clear-on-capture semantics as the div trackers.

Update ex_capture:
  ex_capture = !stall && !div_pipeline_stall && !mul_pipeline_stall

Drive a new top-level output `mul_busy = mul_pipeline_stall`.
Wire alu's start_mul / mul_busy / mul_done ports.

Branch / redirect logic unchanged (no MUL is ever a branch
or jump). Misalign trap logic unchanged.

Add input `mul_busy`. Update stall logic to:
  stall_if      = load_use_hazard || imem_stall || dmem_stall
                  || div_busy || mul_busy
  stall_id      = dmem_stall || load_use_hazard || div_busy
                  || mul_busy
  flush_id      = (load_use_hazard || redirect)
                  && !dmem_stall && !div_busy && !mul_busy
  stall_ex_mem  = dmem_stall || div_busy || mul_busy
  hold_mem_wb   = dmem_stall || div_busy || mul_busy

mul_busy and div_busy are mutually exclusive in this
single-issue core (a single instruction in EX cannot be both
DIV/REM and MUL). The ORs simply union them; no priority
ordering is needed.

Wire ex_stage's new `mul_busy` output into hazard_unit's new
`mul_busy` input via a new `ex_mul_busy` net (mirror of the
existing `ex_div_busy` plumbing). No top-level RVFI changes.

Update the existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU vectors to drive
the new start_mul / mul_busy / mul_done handshake (same
pattern as the _check_div helper added in hyp-002 -- create
a _check_mul helper that sets operands, pulses start_mul for
one cycle, polls mul_done with a small timeout, captures
out). Preserve all current bit-exact RV32M vectors:
  - signed * signed (MULH)
  - unsigned * unsigned (MUL, MULHU)
  - signed * unsigned (MULHSU)
  - sign-extension corner cases (INT_MIN * INT_MIN, etc.)

Add a new test back_to_back_muls that issues five
consecutive multiplies (alternating signed/unsigned ops) to
verify the pipeline register clears cleanly between
operations, mirroring back_to_back_divides from hyp-002.

Add a mixed mul-then-div test to verify the two M-ext
handshakes don't interfere when issued in sequence.

implementation_notes:
# hyp-20260427-003: Pipeline the 32x32 multiplier — implementation notes

## What changed

### New module: `rtl/mul.sv`
Synchronous pipelined 32x32 → 64-bit multiplier. Three RV32M product
flavors selected by `op_sel`:

| `op_sel` | flavor | feeds                |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| `2'd0`   | uu     | `MUL` (low), `MULHU` |
| `2'd1`   | ss     | `MULH`               |
| `2'd2`   | su     | `MULHSU`             |

The `MUL` low half is identical across signedness, so the cheapest
realization (uu) is used for it.

Latency is fixed at 1 cycle:
- cycle 0: `start=1`, `busy=1`, `done=0`. Posedge captures one of three
  combinationally-computed products into `product_q`.
- cycle 1: `busy=1`, `done=1`. Result available on `result_hi/result_lo`.
- cycle 2: returns to IDLE.

The `product_q` flop is the **only** register stage on the path, so
Yosys is free to map it onto the GW2A DSP cluster's internal `M_REG`
during synthesis (the entire combinational cone — sign-extension mux
plus the 64-bit multiply — sits behind that single register, and the
backend gets to choose where to physically place it inside the DSP
block).

No reset on the operand or product datapaths — only `state` resets.
This matches the project pattern where `divu_q` / `quot_q` are also
unreset (see `rtl/divu.sv`).

### Updated `rtl/alu.sv`
- Added ports `start_mul`, `mul_busy`, `mul_done` mirroring the existing
  `start_div / div_busy / div_done` set.
- Removed the three combinational 64-bit products (`mul_uu`, `mul_ss`,
  `mul_su`) and the stand-alone product expressions in the
  `always_comb`. Replaced them with an instance of `mul`.
- Added `mul_op_sel` mux that maps `ALU_MUL/MULHU → uu`, `ALU_MULH → ss`,
  `ALU_MULHSU → su`.
- `out` mux for non-ALTOPS:
  - `ALU_MUL    → mul_result_lo`
  - `ALU_MULH   → mul_result_hi`
  - `ALU_MULHU  → mul_result_hi`
  - `ALU_MULHSU → mul_result_hi`
- Under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`: `mul_busy = 0`, `mul_done = 1` (always
  asserted). The XOR-of-add/sub formulas for MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU are
  preserved exactly as before, so the per-iteration formal gate keeps
  its 20-step BMC budget (riscv-formal §7.6). Added `start_mul` to the
  `_altops_unused` lint-shim so Verilator stays happy.

### Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv`
- New port: `mul_busy` (output to hazard unit / top).
- New flops: `mul_started_q`, `mul_completed_q` mirroring the divider
  pair. They are independent of the divider trackers — a single
  instruction in EX is at most one of {mul, div}, so simultaneous-busy
  is impossible by construction.
- New `is_mul_op`, `start_mul_w`, `mul_pipeline_stall`,
  `alu_mul_busy`, `alu_mul_done` signals.
- `ex_capture` now AND-NOT-includes `mul_pipeline_stall`:
  `ex_capture = !stall && !div_pipeline_stall && !mul_pipeline_stall`.
- ALU instantiation extended with the new mul ports.
- Tracker flop block restructured to two parallel update branches
  (one for div, one for mul), each with its own
  `if (ex_capture && is_*_op) clear else update` shape. Cleaner than
  nesting both into a single `if/else if`.
- Latency line in the header updated.

### Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`
Added `mul_busy` input. ORed into the same five outputs as `div_busy`:
- `stall_if`, `stall_id`, `stall_ex_mem`, `hold_mem_wb`: just `|| mul_busy`.
- `flush_id`: now `&& !mul_busy` in addition to `&& !div_busy`
  (mirror of the divider gate so an in-flight multiply is held, not
  flushed).
- `flush_if` is unchanged — `mul_busy` holds the PC, so IF re-presents
  the same instruction each cycle and no flush is needed.

### Updated `rtl/core.sv`
Added `ex_mul_busy` net mirroring `ex_div_busy`. Wired from
`u_ex.mul_busy` to `u_hazard.mul_busy`. No top-level RVFI changes.

### Updated `test/test_alu.py`
- New `_check_mul` helper (mirrors `_check_div`): asserts `start_mul`,
  polls `mul_done`, captures `out`, drops `start_mul`, clocks once.
- Reset shim adds `dut.start_mul.value = 0` to `_setup`.
- All existing `mul`/`mulh`/`mulhu`/`mulhsu` vectors retargeted to
  `_check_mul` — they preserve the prior bit-exact RV32M expectations
  (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, signed*unsigned, INT_MIN edge cases).
- New `back_to_back_muls` test: 5 consecutive multiplies alternating
  flavors (MUL, MULHU, MULH, MULHSU, MUL) verifies the pipeline
  register clears between operations.
- New `mul_then_div` test: alternating mul/div sequence verifies the
  two M-ext handshakes don't interfere.
- Runner sources list extended with `mul.sv` (between `divu.sv` and
  `alu.sv`).

### Updated `test/test_pipeline.py`
Source list extended with `mul.sv` (the test_alu.py runner globs the
list, but the test_pipeline.py runner has an explicit list and would
fail with `Cannot find module 'mul'` without this addition).

## Verification
- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` — clean.
- Same lint with `-DRISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` — clean.
- `pytest test/test_alu.py` — 26/26 cocotb tests pass (all original
  vectors plus `back_to_back_muls` and `mul_then_div`).
- `pytest test/test_pipeline.py` — 11/11 cocotb tests pass.
- `pytest test/test_decoder.py test/test_imm_gen.py test/test_reg_file.py`
  — 3/3 pass (no regression in unrelated units).

## Deviations from the hypothesis plan

1. **Single shared sign-extend-then-multiply, not three parallel
   multipliers.** The hypothesis described the multiplier as wrapping
   "the three product variants (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned,
   signed*unsigned)" with three parallel products. The implementation
   computes all three combinationally (`prod_uu`, `prod_ss`, `prod_su`)
   and selects the one to register based on `op_sel`. Yosys is expected
   to fold the unused branches when mapping to the GW2A DSPs; if it
   doesn't, the area cost is bounded (three 64-bit multiplies behind a
   3:1 mux) and identical to the *combinational* shape that lived in
   the prior `alu.sv`. Switching to a single sign-extended multiply
   would be a follow-up area optimization, not a correctness change.

2. **Tracker-flop reset shape.** The hypothesis text described
   "extend the existing div handshake to a shared mul/div mechanism"
   suggesting one combined tracker pair. The implementation keeps two
   independent tracker pairs (one for div, one for mul). They cannot
   conflict — an instruction is at most one of {mul, div} — and keeping
   them separate makes the `ex_capture && is_*_op` clear rule a
   straight mirror of the divider pattern, easier to reason about.

3. **`alu_mul_busy` in ex_stage marked unused.** Same treatment as
   `alu_div_busy` — the gating-relevant signal is ex_stage's own
   `mul_pipeline_stall`, computed from `is_mul_op`, the completion
   flop, and `alu_mul_done`. The `alu_mul_busy` output exists for
   completeness / future debug.

4. **`test_pipeline.py` source list update.** Not mentioned in the
   hypothesis but was necessary because the file pins an explicit
   source list. Trivial mechanical change.

5. **Skipped optional 0-cycle bypass for `a==0 || b==0`.** The
   hypothesis explicitly noted this was an optional v1 optimization
   ("the v1 implementation keeps every MUL one-cycle-latency to
   minimize moving parts"). Followed that guidance.

## Concerns

1. **DSP placement is the load-bearing assumption.** The whole
   hypothesis depends on Yosys/nextpnr-himbaechel mapping `product_q`
   into the GW2A DSP cluster's `M_REG`. If it ends up as soft-LUT
   flops *behind* the DSP combinational output, the timing win
   evaporates and we just paid 1 cycle of IPC for nothing. The next
   FPGA report will show whether DSP_M_REG was actually used; if not,
   we can either (a) explicitly instantiate `DSP18` with a register
   attribute or (b) move the operand latches into the always_ff so
   there's a register on the *input* side of the multiplier as well
   (the DSP also has `A_REG`/`B_REG`). Option (b) is the proven Gowin
   pattern but it bumps latency to 2 cycles.

2. **mul_busy / div_busy mutual-exclusivity is enforced by the
   single-issue ISA, not by RTL assertions.** If a future hypothesis
   adds a second issue slot or a uop-cracking decoder where one
   instruction could be both a multiply and a divide, the OR-union in
   the hazard unit and the independent trackers in ex_stage would
   silently work but the simultaneous-active-busy invariant would no
   longer hold. Adding a `mul_busy && div_busy` `assert` would make the
   contract explicit; left out for now to match the existing style of
   the hazard unit (no assertions).

3. **Formal coverage of MUL arithmetic remains in `make formal-deep`
   only.** Per CLAUDE.md, the per-iteration formal gate runs ALTOPS
   mode and substitutes XOR-of-add/sub stand-ins for MUL/DIV/REM. The
   pipelined multiplier's *handshake / stall / order* behavior is
   covered by ALTOPS (where mul_busy=0 / mul_done=1 makes
   mul_pipeline_stall fall through to 0). Bit-exact arithmetic is
   covered by the cocotb `_check_mul` vectors and by the cosim
   end-to-end CoreMark trace; full arithmetic formal proof requires
   `make formal-deep` (slow, run periodically). This matches the
   M-extension contract introduced by hyp-002 for the divider.

4. **Edge-case bypass not implemented.** `a == 0` or `b == 0` always
   takes the 1-cycle latency. If CoreMark's hot loops include a lot of
   `crc_table_init`-style early-zero patterns, we leave a small IPC
   improvement on the table. The hypothesis explicitly defers this to
   a follow-up; recorded here so we remember it's available.

title: Static backward-taken branch prediction at IF to skip the taken-branch bubble
motivation:
After hyp-002 (iterative divider) lifted Fmax from 127 MHz to 152 MHz
and slashed LUT4 from 9563 to 5369, the design is no longer
combinational-arithmetic-bound. hyp-003's attempt to also pipeline the
32x32 multiplier REGRESSED Fmax (152 -> 143 MHz, -6%) and added IPC
cost on every MUL — strong evidence that (a) the multiplier was
already being mapped through the GW2A DSP M_REG by Yosys retiming, and
(b) the new critical path is no longer in the M-extension arithmetic
cones. So the next axis to attack is IPC, not Fmax.

In the current pipeline (5-stage, IF/ID combinational + 4 register
stages), a TAKEN branch costs exactly ONE bubble:

    Cycle N:    branch in EX, redirect=1, IF outputs NOP, ID/EX <= '0
    Cycle N+1:  IF fetches redirect_target, EX has NOP (the bubble)
    Cycle N+2:  EX has redirect_target (target now in flight)

CoreMark v1.0 is loop-dense by construction. Its three hot kernels —
matrix multiply-accumulate (matrix_mul_*), state-machine character
classifier (core_state_transition), and crc16 / list_search inner
loops — are all dominated by short backward branches at the bottom of
small loops. Static prediction of "every backward branch is taken" is
the textbook heuristic; on classical SPECint and CoreMark traces it
hits ~85-95% accuracy because virtually every backward branch is a
loop backedge taken N times before falling through.

Conservative IPC accounting from the hyp-002 baseline:
  - Cycles per iter: 449,182  (4,491,817 / 10)
  - Estimated dynamic instr/iter: ~270K (based on ~0.6 IPC for an
    in-order RV32IM with no caches and a 1-cycle branch bubble)
  - Branch fraction:        ~15%   (~40K branches per iter)
  - Taken fraction:         ~60%   (loops dominate dynamic counts)
  - Backward-taken / total taken: ~95% (forward branches are mostly
    conditional fall-through)
  - Backward-taken correctly predicted at 90%: ~21K cycles saved/iter
  - Net IPC headroom: 21K / 449K ≈ 4.6%

The gain is modest in isolation but FREE in two senses:
  1. Zero IPC cost on the 90%+ correctly-predicted path.
  2. No structural change to the rest of the pipeline. The redirect
     priority already gates correctly when EX has to override a wrong
     prediction.

Why static (not a 2-bit BHT or BTB) for v1: a BHT/BTB requires a
RAM-style structure indexed at IF time, plus an update writeback path
from EX. The static predictor is purely combinational on imem_data
(already available in IF) plus the existing PC adder. It adds one
32-bit adder and a 3:1 mux on the next_pc path — well below the
decoder + regfile cone that already lives in the IF/ID combinational
cycle. If this hypothesis lands cleanly, a follow-up can add a small
BHT to also catch FORWARD-taken branches (~5% additional uplift in
expectation).

Why not move branch resolve to ID: that's a structural change with
knock-on effects (need EX/MEM/WB-to-ID forwarding for branches that
consume a recent register write). Out of scope here. Static prediction
is the minimum-risk first cut.
hypothesis:
Add a static "backward-taken" branch predictor to the IF stage:

  1. IF combinationally inspects the freshly-fetched imem_data:
       predict_taken_w = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1100011)  // BRANCH opcode
                      && imem_data[31];                  // negative imm
     The B-type immediate is reconstructed locally:
       predict_imm = {{19{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31], imem_data[7],
                      imem_data[30:25], imem_data[11:8], 1'b0};
     Predicted target:
       predict_target = pc + predict_imm;

  2. The PC update mux gains a third input. Priority order:
       redirect       (EX-stage actual resolve, highest)
       predict_taken  (IF-stage static prediction)
       pc + 4         (sequential default)
     The IF stage drives PC <= predict_target on the cycle a backward
     branch is fetched (and no redirect/stall is in effect).

  3. The IF/ID bundle (if_id_t in core_pkg.sv) gains one bit
     `predicted_taken` carried alongside pc/instr/valid. ID stage
     passes it through into a new id_ex_t.predicted_taken field
     (a peer of `ctrl`, not inside ctrl_t — it's a pipeline carry,
     not a decoded control signal). Wrong-path predictions naturally
     come through with valid=0 (flush) and predicted_taken=0 because
     the IF combinational output is forced to NOP on flush/redirect.

  4. EX stage uses the prediction to suppress redundant redirects.
     New mispredict logic:
       actual_taken    = in.ctrl.is_branch && branch_cond;
       mispredict      = in.ctrl.is_branch
                       && (in.predicted_taken != actual_taken);
       // For a CORRECT taken prediction, no redirect is needed —
       // the target is already in IF/ID/EX from the prediction.
       // For a CORRECT not-taken prediction, no redirect either.
       // For a mispredict, the redirect target is:
       //   actual_taken  -> branch_target (was predicted not-taken)
       //   !actual_taken -> pc + 4        (was predicted taken)
       redirect_branch        = mispredict;
       redirect_target_branch = actual_taken ? branch_target
                                             : (in.pc + 32'd4);

     Misalign trap logic stays the same (suppresses the redirect, sets
     is_illegal). Combined with jumps (still always redirect):
       redirect = ((mispredict && in.ctrl.is_branch) || in.ctrl.is_jump)
                && !misalign_fault;
       redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jump ? jump_target
                       : actual_taken    ? branch_target
                                         : (in.pc + 32'd4);

RVFI INVARIANTS PRESERVED:
  - #1 (port set):          unchanged
  - #2 (rvfi_trap=illegal): unchanged — branch traps still flow
                            through the existing misalign + ctrl_t
                            path
  - #4 (rvfi_order +1):     unchanged — branch retires once at MEM/WB
                            with order N, target retires next with
                            order N+1. Wrong-path predictions never
                            retire (valid=0 on flush).
  - #5 (liveness):          unchanged — the predictor is purely
                            combinational, no new state machine. Any
                            symbolic instruction stream still makes
                            forward progress: EX always resolves the
                            branch and either confirms or redirects.
  - #5b (M-ext bit-exact):  untouched
  - #6-#10:                 untouched

Critically for riscv-formal's `causal` / `pc_fwd` checks: the retired
branch's pc_wdata comes from EX's actual_taken, not from the
prediction. A mispredict generates a redirect that flushes wrong-path
in-flight instructions before they retire. So the retirement stream's
pc_rdata == predecessor's pc_wdata invariant holds bit-exactly.

TIMING ARGUMENT:
  - IF stage adds: opcode equality check (1 LUT), B-type imm
    assembly (wires), 32-bit adder pc+imm (carry chain, ~1 ns), 3:1
    mux on next_pc (~1 LUT level). Total ~2-3 LUT levels of new
    depth on the PC-reg input path.
  - The existing IF+decoder+regfile-read path (the imem_data ->
    decoder case -> ID/EX cone, parallel to PC update) already
    dominates. The new prediction logic does NOT extend that path.
  - The EX-stage redirect path is unchanged in length: same forward
    mux, same comparator, same target adder. We just AND-gate the
    redirect signal with `mispredict`, which is one extra 2-input
    AND/XOR gate.
  - Expected Fmax delta: 0 to -2% (worst case if the new IF adder
    pushes PC-reg input critical at 152 MHz). Net fitness still
    positive given the IPC win.

TEST UPDATES:
  - test/test_pipeline.py: add test cases that issue a tight
    backward branch loop (BNE x1, x0, -4) and verify (a) the loop
    retires correctly, (b) rvfi_order increments by exactly N over
    N iterations, (c) no spurious double-retirement when the
    predictor and EX agree. Add a forward-mispredict case (forward
    BEQ taken) that confirms EX still redirects correctly when the
    predictor said "not taken".
  - test/test_alu.py: no changes needed (predictor is in IF, ALU
    untouched).

changes.description:
Add a `predicted_taken` 1-bit field to the if_id_t struct and to
the id_ex_t struct (as a peer of `ctrl`, NOT inside ctrl_t —
this is a pipeline-carry bit, not a decoded control signal).

Position the new field at the END of each struct so the existing
packed bit ordering for previously-defined fields stays intact
and any unrelated formal/cosim pattern matching on legacy
offsets keeps working. Default value via '0 initialization in
reset paths is naturally 0 (= "not predicted taken").

Header comment: document that the predicted_taken bit is the
contract by which IF tells EX "I redirected speculatively to
pc+imm; please confirm or override". A 0 means IF chose pc+4;
EX should redirect on actual_taken=1 (legacy behavior). A 1
means IF chose pc+imm; EX should redirect on actual_taken=0
(the new mispredict path).

Add static "backward-taken" branch prediction. Combinationally
derive from imem_data:

  is_branch_op   = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1100011);
  b_imm          = {{19{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31],
                    imem_data[7], imem_data[30:25],
                    imem_data[11:8], 1'b0};
  predict_taken  = is_branch_op && imem_data[31];
  predict_target = pc + b_imm;

Update the next_pc mux to a 3:1 priority chain:
  next_pc = redirect      ? redirect_target
          : predict_taken ? predict_target
                          : pc + 32'd4;

PC reg synchronous update gains the predict_taken branch on the
same priority below redirect (redirect always wins, then
predict_taken, then pc+4 — and stall holds the PC if no
redirect).

The if_id_t output:
  out.pc              = pc
  out.instr           = (flush || redirect) ? NOP : imem_data
  out.valid           = !(flush || redirect)
  out.predicted_taken = !(flush || redirect) && predict_taken

Forcing predicted_taken=0 on flush/redirect is critical: a
wrong-path prediction must not flow downstream as a
"predicted-taken" carry, or EX would compute mispredict
incorrectly when the genuine retired branch reaches it.

Header-comment update: latency unchanged; document the new
static predictor and its prediction-correctness contract with
EX (mispredict-only redirects).

Pass `predicted_taken` from the IF/ID bundle into the id_ex_t
register on a parallel path to the existing pc/instr/rs1/rs2
flow. ID stage doesn't decode or modify the bit — it just
pipelines it.

In the always_ff register block: add
  reg_q.predicted_taken <= in.predicted_taken;
under the !stall && !reset && !flush branch. On reset/flush
reg_q goes to '0, so predicted_taken naturally clears to 0
(= "no prediction in flight").

No changes to decoder, imm_gen, regfile read addresses, or any
other ID-stage logic. The new field is a pure pipeline carry.

Use the in-flight `predicted_taken` to suppress redirect on
correctly-predicted branches and redirect-with-correct-target
on mispredicted ones.

Add:
  actual_taken = in.ctrl.is_branch && branch_cond;
  mispredict   = in.ctrl.is_branch
              && (in.predicted_taken != actual_taken);

Replace the existing `branch_taken` usage in the redirect
computation. The redirect signal becomes:

  redirect_branch_or_jump = (in.ctrl.is_branch && mispredict)
                          || in.ctrl.is_jump;
  redirect = redirect_branch_or_jump && !misalign_fault;
  redirect_target =
        in.ctrl.is_jump  ? jump_target
      : actual_taken     ? branch_target
                         : (in.pc + 32'd4);

The EX/MEM register's pc_next field continues to be the
ARCHITECTURALLY CORRECT next PC (actual_taken ? branch_target :
pc+4) — RVFI cares about what actually retired, not what was
predicted. branch_taken in the captured ex_mem_t is set from
actual_taken (rename for clarity) so any downstream RVFI
consumer reads the truth.

Misalign-trap logic: a mispredicted branch whose ACTUAL target
is misaligned still traps. The existing misalign_branch logic
uses (is_branch && actual_taken && target[1:0] != 0); rename
branch_taken -> actual_taken in those expressions. Misalign
suppresses the redirect (PC stays linear), same as today.

No change to the divider/multiplier handshake, the EX/MEM
register layout (apart from renaming branch_taken to
actual_taken inside the always_ff block), or the forwarding
muxes.

Add three test cases under the existing cocotb runner:

1. test_backward_branch_prediction_correct
   - Program: 5-instruction tight loop driven by BNE x1, x0, -4
     with x1 initialized to 5; loop counts down via ADDI.
     Exit when x1 == 0.
   - Run for ~50 cycles, assert rvfi_order increments at the
     expected rate (one retirement per cycle for the steady-state
     loop body, with the FALLING-THROUGH branch still costing 1
     cycle of bubble).
   - Critical assertion: the bubble count is N (one per loop
     iteration falling through after the last predicted-taken
     backward branch resolves), NOT 2*N (which would be the
     pre-prediction behavior).

2. test_forward_branch_mispredict
   - Program: forward BEQ that's actually taken (rare but
     legal). Predictor says "not taken" (because imm[31]=0);
     EX detects mispredict and redirects.
   - Assert: target instruction retires correctly (rvfi_order
     +1 over the branch), pc_wdata at branch retirement equals
     the actual taken target, no double-retire.

3. test_backward_branch_mispredict
   - Program: backward BNE that's NOT taken (e.g. setup so the
     condition is false on first encounter). Predictor says
     "taken"; EX detects mispredict and redirects to pc+4.
   - Assert: pc+4 instruction retires after branch, rvfi_order
     +1, no double-retire, no x-state.

No changes needed to test_alu.py, test_decoder.py,
test_imm_gen.py, or test_reg_file.py — predictor lives at IF
and EX boundary, doesn't touch ALU/decoder/imm_gen/regfile.

title: JAL target prediction at IF to skip the unconditional-jump bubble
motivation:
Standing at fitness 339.02 (hyp-002), the next axis to attack is IPC,
not Fmax. hyp-003 confirmed Yosys/nextpnr already retimed the 32x32
multiplier into the GW2A DSP M_REG (Fmax actually fell when we tried
to hand-pipeline it). hyp-004 attempted static backward-taken branch
prediction and broke formal: insn_beq/bne/blt/bge/bltu/bgeu/pc_fwd
all failed at BMC depth 20, almost certainly because the
`predicted_taken` bit interacted with the mispredict path in EX in a
way that corrupted the EX/MEM `pc_next` (= rvfi_pc_wdata) contract
for at least one branch direction.

This hypothesis proposes the strictly-simpler subset of IF-stage
prediction that hyp-004 attempted: predict only **unconditional
jumps (JAL)**, never branches, never JALR. The reasoning:

1. JAL is unconditional. There is no "predicted-taken vs predicted-
   not-taken" axis, no register-dependent comparator to disagree
   with at EX. The architectural target is pc + j_imm, computable
   in IF combinationally from imem_data alone (no rs1/rs2 needed).
   EX's resolve will compute the exact same target -- there is no
   mispredict-direction case to handle.

2. Today every JAL costs exactly one bubble in the 5-stage pipeline:

     Cycle N:    JAL fetched, in IF/ID
     Cycle N+1:  JAL in ID/EX, IF fetches PC+4 (wrong path)
     Cycle N+2:  JAL in EX/MEM, EX asserts redirect -> PC = target,
                 IF/ID and ID/EX flushed to NOP
     Cycle N+3:  IF fetches the JAL target

   With IF-side JAL prediction the target is in flight at cycle N+1
   and the bubble disappears.

3. JAL is NOT rare in CoreMark. CoreMark v1.0 6 KB build is a
   call-heavy workload: list_search, matrix_test, core_state, crc16,
   and the per-iter dispatch in core_main all use small leaf
   functions. Reading the prior trace (4,491,817 cycles / 10 iters
   ~= 449K cycles/iter at ~0.6 IPC -> ~270K dynamic instr/iter), the
   dynamic JAL fraction is typically 3-6% of instructions in
   CoreMark on RV32 (function-call density), so call this ~10K JALs
   per iter. Eliminating one bubble each yields ~10K cycles back per
   iter, or ~2.2% IPC. That's a free win at no Fmax cost (predictor
   is purely combinational on imem_data and the existing pc + imm
   adder is already the IF-stage carry chain).

Critically for safety: this is the strict subset of hyp-004's change
that does NOT touch branches. The branch comparator in EX is
unmodified; insn_beq/bne/blt/bge/bltu/bgeu formal checks are exactly
the proven-good code from hyp-002. The only difference vs the
current core is JAL's redirect from EX is conditionally suppressed
when IF already redirected.
hypothesis:
Add a single-cycle, non-speculative, 100%-accurate JAL target
predictor to the IF stage. JAL has opcode 7'b1101111; the J-type
immediate is fully embedded in the instruction word; the target
is pc + j_imm. The mechanism:

1. IF combinationally inspects imem_data each cycle:
     is_jal_w   = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111);
     j_imm_w    = {{11{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31],
                   imem_data[19:12], imem_data[20],
                   imem_data[30:21], 1'b0};
     jal_target = pc + j_imm_w;
     predict_jal = is_jal_w && !flush && !redirect;

   "!flush && !redirect" prevents wrong-path imem_data from
   triggering a prediction. The same gating is already in place
   for instr forwarding (existing IF emits NOP on flush/redirect).

2. PC-update mux gains a third arm. Priority order is
   redirect > predict_jal > pc+4. The RTL update (redirect at top
   priority) keeps the existing dmem-stall / divide-stall semantics
   -- if any stall is asserted, predict_jal does NOT update PC
   (re-evaluated next cycle once the stall clears).

3. The IF/ID combinational bundle (if_id_t) gains a 1-bit
   `predicted_jal` field. ID stage carries it into a new id_ex_t
   `predicted_jal` field (peer of `ctrl`, NOT inside ctrl_t -- a
   pipeline-carry bit, not a decoded control signal). On flush /
   redirect, predicted_jal is forced to 0 in the IF combinational
   output, identical handling to the existing `valid` bit.

4. EX stage uses `predicted_jal` to suppress the redundant redirect
   for predicted JALs only:

     redirect_jump_actual = in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.predicted_jal;
     redirect = (branch_taken || redirect_jump_actual) && !misalign_fault;

   The redirect_target mux is unchanged:
     redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target;

   `branch_taken` (and therefore branch redirects) are completely
   untouched by this hypothesis -- the entire branch path stays
   bit-identical to the proven hyp-002 implementation.

   Why this is safe in the JALR-via-mispredicted-path case: a JALR
   can only have predicted_jal=1 if IF mistakenly tagged a non-JAL
   opcode as JAL, which is impossible -- the opcode check is exact.
   A JALR with predicted_jal=0 takes the legacy redirect path.
   Any in-flight wrong-path instruction (e.g. IF speculatively
   fetched the next sequential after JAL while EX flushes wider)
   gets predicted_jal=0 in the IF stage's output (because either
   valid is forced low or the decoder will identify it as something
   other than JAL).

5. The EX/MEM register's pc_next field continues to be the
   ARCHITECTURALLY CORRECT next PC, computed from EX's standard
   resolve logic (jump_target for JAL). RVFI sees rvfi_pc_wdata =
   jump_target at JAL retirement, identical to today. The next
   retired instruction's pc_rdata is jump_target (it was fetched
   from jump_target one cycle earlier than today, but the value
   captured into mem_wb.pc is the same). pc_fwd_ch0 holds.

RVFI / formal invariants:

  - #1 (port set):       unchanged
  - #2 (rvfi_trap):      unchanged (JAL is legal)
  - #4 (rvfi_order +1):  unchanged -- JAL retires once with order N,
                         target retires next with order N+1
  - #5 (liveness):       unchanged -- predictor is purely
                         combinational, no new state, no new
                         termination obligations
  - #5b (M-ext):         untouched
  - #6-#10:              untouched

Why this avoids hyp-004's failure mode:

  - hyp-004 carried a `predicted_taken` bit AND modified the EX
    redirect to mispredict-only for branches AND modified pc_next
    computation to depend on actual_taken. The formal failures
    were on insn_b{eq,ne,...} and pc_fwd, all branch-side checks.

  - This hypothesis touches NEITHER the branch_taken signal NOR the
    branch path through EX/MEM/WB. The branch comparator, the
    branch_target adder, the branch_taken-driven pc_next, and the
    misalign_branch trap logic are byte-identical to hyp-002.

  - The only new behavior in EX is gating `is_jump` with
    `!predicted_jal`. This affects the redirect signal only, never
    the EX/MEM register's data fields (pc_next, alu_result, ctrl,
    etc.). RVFI fields are derived exclusively from EX/MEM register
    data, so no rvfi_* assertion can be reached by this change
    unless a wrong-path instruction sneaks past the IF flush --
    which is structurally impossible because flush_if and
    redirect both force IF/ID to NOP via existing logic.

  - JAL never has a "wrong direction" mispredict (it's always
    taken), so the hyp-004 mispredict-redirect-target ambiguity
    that may have corrupted pc_wdata simply does not exist here.

Timing:

  - IF adds: opcode-equality check (1 LUT), J-imm wire-permutation
    (free), 32-bit pc + j_imm adder (1 carry chain, ~1 ns on GW2A),
    a 3:1 mux on next_pc (~1 LUT level). Total ~2-3 LUT levels of
    new depth, in parallel with the existing decoder + regfile read
    cone (which is the dominant ID-stage cone today).
  - EX adds: a single AND gate (`is_jump && !predicted_jal`) on
    the redirect path. Negligible.
  - Expected Fmax delta: 0 to -2% worst case, far smaller than the
    IPC win.

Test updates:

  - test/test_pipeline.py: add a tight JAL loop (JAL x0, -4)
    verifying rvfi_order increments at one retirement per cycle in
    steady state (no bubble), and a JAL-then-add sequence verifying
    the target instruction lands correctly without a bubble.
  - test/test_alu.py: no changes (predictor is at IF/EX boundary,
    ALU untouched).
  - test/test_decoder.py: no changes (JAL detection in IF is opcode-
    level, doesn't touch the central decoder).

changes.description:
Add a 1-bit `predicted_jal` field to the if_id_t struct AND to
the id_ex_t struct, positioned at the END of each struct so the
existing packed bit ordering for previously-defined fields is
undisturbed.

Field semantics: 1 means "IF redirected speculatively to
pc + j_imm because this instruction looked like JAL; EX should
suppress its redundant redirect". 0 means "IF made no
prediction; EX should redirect as normal (legacy behavior)".
Default (via '0 reset / flush) is 0 -- the safe legacy path.

Document in the header comment that this is a pipeline-carry
bit, NOT a decoded control bit, and is therefore not inside
ctrl_t. (Same rationale would have applied to hyp-004's
predicted_taken; including this discipline in the comment
reduces ambiguity for future hypotheses.)

Add JAL target prediction. Combinationally derived from
imem_data and current pc:

  is_jal_w     = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111);
  j_imm_w      = {{11{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31],
                  imem_data[19:12], imem_data[20],
                  imem_data[30:21], 1'b0};
  jal_target_w = pc + j_imm_w;
  predict_jal  = is_jal_w && !(flush || redirect);

Update the next_pc mux to a 3:1 priority chain:
  next_pc = redirect    ? redirect_target
          : predict_jal ? jal_target_w
                        : pc + 32'd4;

PC reg synchronous update gains the predict_jal arm at the same
priority below redirect (redirect always wins, then predict_jal,
then pc+4 -- gated by !stall as today). Stall holds the PC
regardless of predict_jal so dmem-stall / divide-stall
semantics are preserved exactly (re-evaluate next cycle once
the stall clears).

The if_id_t output:
  out.pc            = pc
  out.instr         = (flush || redirect) ? 32'h0000_0013 : imem_data
  out.valid         = !(flush || redirect)
  out.predicted_jal = !(flush || redirect) && predict_jal

Forcing predicted_jal=0 on flush/redirect mirrors the existing
`valid` gating and is critical: a wrong-path imem_data must
not flow downstream as "predicted-jal" or EX would suppress a
legitimate redirect on the genuine retired instruction.

Header comment update: latency unchanged (PC reg sync, output
combinational); document the new JAL predictor and the
contract bit predicted_jal.

Pass `predicted_jal` from the IF/ID bundle into the id_ex_t
register on a parallel path to pc/instr/rs1/rs2. ID does not
decode or modify the bit -- it is a pure pipeline carry.

In the always_ff register block, under the !stall && !flush &&
!reset branch, add:
  reg_q.predicted_jal <= in.predicted_jal;
On reset / flush the entire reg_q clears to '0, so
predicted_jal naturally goes to 0 (= "no prediction in
flight") -- this is the safe default and matches the hyp-004
treatment for predicted_taken.

No changes to decoder, imm_gen, regfile read addresses, or any
other ID-stage logic.

Use the in-flight `predicted_jal` to suppress the redundant
redirect on JALs that were already redirected at IF.

Add:
  redirect_jump_actual = in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.predicted_jal;
Replace the existing redirect computation:
  redirect = (branch_taken || redirect_jump_actual) && !misalign_fault;

No other change. In particular:
  - branch_taken is unchanged (full hyp-002 behavior).
  - redirect_target is unchanged: it still routes
    jump_target for is_jump and branch_target for branches.
  - misalign_branch / misalign_jump trap logic is unchanged --
    a predicted JAL whose target is misaligned still traps via
    ctrl_with_trap (is_illegal=1, reg_write=0). The redirect
    would have been suppressed by misalign_fault anyway, so
    predicted_jal=1 doesn't widen the trap surface.
  - The EX/MEM register's pc_next field is unchanged -- it
    continues to be jump_target for is_jump, branch_target for
    taken branches, pc+4 otherwise. The architecturally-correct
    retirement value flows to RVFI exactly as today.

Add a 1-line lint comment documenting why predicted_jal must
remain a peer of ctrl (not inside ctrl_t): a decoded control
signal can be flushed to '0 and no behavior changes; a wrongly-
flushed predicted_jal could in principle leave EX redirect
suppressed for a real wrong-path JAL that needed redirect.
The IF stage's flush gating prevents this case in practice,
but the discipline matters.

Add three test cases under the existing cocotb runner:

1. test_jal_target_prediction
   - Program: JAL x0, +12 at PC=0x00, three NOP instructions
     at PC=0x04..0x0C, ADDI x1, x0, 0x42 at PC=0x0C (the JAL
     target).
   - Run for ~10 cycles. Observe rvfi sequence:
      * The JAL retires with rvfi_pc_wdata == 0x0C.
      * The ADDI retires with rvfi_pc_rdata == 0x0C, rvfi_order
        == JAL's order + 1.
      * The PC of the in-flight instruction at cycle JAL+2 (one
        cycle after JAL enters ID/EX) is 0x10 (= 0x0C + 4),
        proving the IF redirected the cycle after fetching
        JAL.
   - Critical assertion: total cycles between JAL fetch and
     ADDI retire is 4 (= IF -> ID -> EX -> MEM -> WB) with
     NO bubble, vs 5 cycles in the pre-prediction core.

2. test_tight_jal_loop
   - Program: JAL x0, -4 at PC=0x04, with PC=0x00 being a NOP
     setup. The JAL targets itself in steady state (its imm
     is -4).
   - Run for ~50 cycles. Assert rvfi_order increments by ~1
     per cycle in steady state (after pipeline fill), proving
     the JAL bubble has been eliminated.

3. test_jal_with_register_writeback
   - Program: JAL x1, +8 at PC=0x00 (a function call), instr
     at PC=0x08 sets x2 = x1.
   - Run for ~10 cycles. Assert (a) JAL writes pc+4 (= 0x04)
     into x1, (b) the ADD-from-x1 instruction at the JAL target
     sees x1 == 0x04 via the existing forwarding path, (c) no
     double-retire of either instruction, (d) rvfi_pc_wdata
     on the JAL is 0x08.

No changes needed to test_alu.py, test_decoder.py,
test_imm_gen.py, or test_reg_file.py -- the JAL predictor
lives entirely between IF and the EX redirect signal, and does
not touch ALU/decoder/imm_gen/regfile.

title: Static JAL target prediction at IF with implicit EX-side discrimination
motivation:
Standing at fitness 339.02 (hyp-002, the divider win), the next axis to
attack is IPC. hyp-003 confirmed Yosys/nextpnr already retimed the 32x32
multiplier into the GW2A DSP M_REG (Fmax actually fell when we tried to
hand-pipeline it, -6%). hyp-004 (static backward-taken branch prediction)
and hyp-005 (static JAL prediction) BOTH broke formal on the same set of
checks: insn_beq/bne/blt/bge/bltu/bgeu/pc_fwd_ch0 at BMC depth 20. Both
attempts added a `predicted_*` 1-bit pipeline carry to if_id_t and
id_ex_t. The shared failure mode is suspicious: branches were
*supposed* to be untouched in hyp-005 (only JAL's redirect was
suppressed) yet branch checks still failed. The likely cause is the
packed-struct extension itself, or the flush/redirect ordering of the
new bit interacting with the stalled-divide / load-use paths in a way
the design notes did not anticipate.

This hypothesis revisits JAL target prediction with a strictly simpler
implementation that avoids the failure mode entirely:

  1. NO new fields in if_id_t or id_ex_t (no struct changes anywhere).
  2. NO changes to core_pkg.sv, id_stage.sv, hazard_unit.sv, or core.sv.
  3. The "predicted_jal" decision is derived IMPLICITLY in EX from the
     JAL opcode itself: any JAL that reaches EX with valid=1 must have
     been predicted at IF. The proof is an IF-side invariant (below).
  4. Misaligned JAL targets (J-immediate bit 1 set) are NOT predicted
     at IF, so the misalign trap path stays byte-identical to the
     proven hyp-002 code. This avoids the only RVFI corner where a
     prediction could create a pc_fwd mismatch.

Why JAL-only (not branches): branches require *direction* prediction,
which is structurally harder (BHT needs state, training, mispredict
redirect target depends on actual_taken). JAL is unconditional and
deterministically computable from imem_data + pc -- no register reads,
no comparator, no mispredict-direction case. This is the strict subset
of hyp-005's design that has no interaction with the branch checker.

Why this avoids hyp-004/005's failures:
  - The branch comparator, branch_target adder, branch_taken signal,
    and the misalign_branch trap logic are all BYTE-IDENTICAL to
    hyp-002. The only EX change is a 1-line redirect formula.
  - No packed-struct extension means no risk of formal binding
    shifting or some downstream tool reading shifted bit positions.
  - The "implicit predicted_jal" rule removes the only path by which
    a wrong-path predicted_jal could leak into ID/EX during an
    already-in-flight redirect: there IS no carry bit, so there is no
    bit to leak.

IPC sizing (from prior trace 4,491,817 cycles / 10 iters):
  - Estimated dynamic instr/iter: ~270K (~0.6 IPC)
  - JAL fraction: ~3-6% (CoreMark is call-heavy: list_search,
    matrix_test, core_state, crc16, leaf calls in core_main)
  - JALs/iter: ~10K
  - Cycles saved: 1 bubble per JAL = ~10K cycles/iter
  - Net IPC: ~10K / 449K = ~2.2%
  - Fitness delta at 152 MHz (Fmax assumed unchanged or +/-1%): +2-3%
hypothesis:
Add a static, 100%-correct JAL target predictor at the IF stage,
combined with a one-line modification to EX that suppresses the now-
redundant JAL redirect. No new pipeline state, no struct changes.

IF-side change (rtl/if_stage.sv):

  Combinationally inspect imem_data each cycle:

    is_jal_w   = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111);
    jal_b1_w   = imem_data[21];                  // J-imm bit 1
    j_imm_w    = {{11{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31],
                  imem_data[19:12], imem_data[20],
                  imem_data[30:21], 1'b0};
    jal_target_w = pc + j_imm_w;
    // Skip prediction when target would be misaligned. The check is
    // (jal_target_w[1:0] != 0) but since pc[1:0] is invariantly 00
    // in this design (proof in the IF header comment below) and
    // j_imm[0] is hardwired 0, we only need to inspect imem_data[21]
    // -- this avoids putting jal_target_w[1] (the adder output) on
    // the predict_jal gating cone.
    predict_jal = is_jal_w && !(flush || redirect) && !jal_b1_w;

  Update the next_pc mux to a 3:1 priority chain (redirect wins,
  then predict_jal, then sequential):

    next_pc = redirect    ? redirect_target
            : predict_jal ? jal_target_w
                          : pc + 32'd4;

  PC reg synchronous update gains the predict_jal arm. Stall holds
  the PC unconditionally (preserves dmem-stall / divide-stall
  semantics). Redirect always wins to preserve the EX redirect
  contract for branches and JALR.

  The if_id_t output bundle is UNCHANGED -- pc/instr/valid only,
  no new field. instr forwarding keeps its existing flush/redirect
  NOP gate (`(flush||redirect) ? 0x00000013 : imem_data`).

EX-side change (rtl/ex_stage.sv): one-line redirect formula.

  Today:
    redirect = (branch_taken || in.ctrl.is_jump) && !misalign_fault;

  New:
    redirect = (branch_taken
                || (in.ctrl.is_jump && in.ctrl.is_jalr))
                && !misalign_fault;

  Behavior table (all four cases, new vs legacy):

    | Instruction        | is_jump | is_jalr | branch_taken | redirect (legacy) | redirect (new) |
    |--------------------|---------|---------|--------------|-------------------|----------------|
    | JAL                | 1       | 0       | 0            | !misalign         | 0              |
    | JALR               | 1       | 1       | 0            | !misalign         | !misalign      |
    | BRANCH (taken)     | 0       | 0       | 1            | !misalign         | !misalign      |
    | BRANCH (not taken) | 0       | 0       | 0            | 0                 | 0              |
    | non-branch/jump    | 0       | 0       | 0            | 0                 | 0              |

  Only the JAL row changes. Everything else is bit-for-bit identical
  to hyp-002. redirect_target, jump_target, branch_target,
  misalign_branch, misalign_jump, ctrl_with_trap (the
  is_illegal/reg_write zero-out on trap), branch_cond, and the EX/MEM
  register's pc_next field are all UNCHANGED.

THE IF-SIDE INVARIANT (the heart of why this works without a carry bit):

  Claim: any JAL that reaches ID/EX with valid=1 has had IF redirect
         PC to its jump_target.

  Proof:
    (a) For JAL to be in ID/EX with valid=1, the ID stage must have
        captured it from IF/ID combinational. Capture happens when
        flush_id=0 and stall_id=0 (per id_stage.sv).
    (b) IF/ID combinational has valid=1 only when flush_if=0
        (per if_stage.sv: `valid = !(flush || redirect)`).
    (c) Therefore at the cycle JAL was in IF/ID-valid, flush_if=0
        AND redirect=0.
    (d) predict_jal_w = is_jal && !(flush||redirect) && !jal_b1.
        With (c), predict_jal_w = is_jal && !jal_b1 = 1 && !jal_b1.
    (e) Sub-claim: jal_b1 (= imm[1] = instr[21]) is 0 for any JAL
        that reaches EX. Because:
        - If jal_b1=1, predict_jal=0 (per (d)).
        - With predict_jal=0 and the JAL fetched, PC continues
          sequentially. The JAL flows through ID/EX legally.
        - In EX, misalign_jump_w detects (jump_target[1:0] != 0)
          and asserts is_illegal=1 / suppresses redirect. The JAL
          traps and pc_next=pc+4. The next-fetched instr (PC+4 from
          IF's sequential update) retires next; pc_fwd matches.
        - So a jal_b1=1 JAL still works correctly via the legacy
          misalign trap -- it just was not predicted. EX's new
          redirect formula gives `(0 || (1 && 0)) && !misalign = 0`,
          same as legacy under misalign (which was `(0 || 1) && 0
          = 0`). Identical behavior.
    (f) For jal_b1=0 JALs (the predicted ones): predict_jal_w=1,
        PC <= jal_target at end of the IF cycle (gated only by
        stall_if; PC eventually moves once any stall clears, and
        ID will not capture a JAL with valid=1 until PC has moved
        per the IF/ID-valid && capture conditions in (a),(b)).

  Corollary: in EX, the in-flight JAL (ctrl.is_jump=1 && is_jalr=0)
  has *already* had its target reached at IF. EX must NOT redirect.
  The new redirect formula does exactly this.

  Edge case (stalls): during dmem_stall or div_busy, stall_if=1 holds
  PC. predict_jal_w stays asserted (flush_if is NOT set by these
  stalls). When the stall clears, PC moves to jal_target_w on the
  SAME cycle ID captures the JAL into ID/EX. Verified by walking the
  ID/EX capture rule (`!stall && !flush`) against the IF PC update
  rule (`!stall && !redirect`).

  Edge case (wrong-path JAL during a branch redirect): when EX
  redirects (branch taken), flush_if=1 propagates. predict_jal_w=0
  (gated on !flush). PC follows redirect_target. The wrong-path JAL
  is NOP'd in IF/ID with valid=0 and never reaches ID/EX. So the
  invariant holds vacuously.

RVFI / FORMAL INVARIANTS:

  - #1 (port set):       UNCHANGED (no new top-level signals).
  - #2 (rvfi_trap):      UNCHANGED. Misaligned JAL still traps via
                         the existing misalign_jump path. The trap
                         condition and ctrl_with_trap mux are
                         byte-identical.
  - #3 (EBREAK only legal SYSTEM): UNCHANGED.
  - #4 (rvfi_order +1):  UNCHANGED. JAL retires once at MEM/WB with
                         order N; the target retires next with
                         N+1. With prediction, the target arrives
                         one cycle earlier physically, but
                         rvfi_order increments per `mem_wb_w.valid`
                         which still goes high once per JAL and
                         once per target -- no double-retire.
  - #5 (liveness):       UNCHANGED. The predictor is purely
                         combinational on imem_data. No new state,
                         no new termination obligations. EX still
                         drives IF to a definite next-PC every cycle.
  - #5b (M-ext):         UNTOUCHED (alu.sv, divu.sv, mul.sv all
                         untouched).
  - #6-#10:              UNTOUCHED (memory bounds, CoreMark CRC,
                         timing markers, decoder default-illegal).

  pc_fwd_ch0 specifically: rvfi_pc_wdata is `mem_wb_w.pc_next`.
  `pc_next` is computed in EX exactly as today (no change to the
  pc_next assignment in ex_stage's always_ff). So for every
  retirement, pc_wdata = the architecturally-correct next PC. The
  next retired instr's pc_rdata = `mem_wb_w.pc`, which is the PC
  at which the target was fetched. With prediction, the target is
  fetched at PC=jump_target (because IF redirected to jump_target),
  so its pc_rdata = jump_target = the JAL's pc_wdata. pc_fwd holds.

TIMING:

  - IF adds: opcode-equality (1 LUT), J-imm wire-permutation (free),
    32-bit pc + j_imm adder (1 carry chain, ~1 ns on GW2A LUT4
    fabric), 3:1 mux on next_pc (~1 LUT level). Total ~2-3 LUT
    levels of new depth on the PC-reg input path. Parallel to (NOT
    gating) the existing decoder + regfile-read cone.
  - EX changes: replace `in.ctrl.is_jump` with
    `(in.ctrl.is_jump && in.ctrl.is_jalr)` -- one extra AND gate.
    Negligible.
  - Expected Fmax delta: 0 to -2% worst case. The 152 MHz Fmax from
    hyp-002 leaves enough slack for a 2-3 LUT-level adder/mux
    addition on a path that wasn't the bottleneck.
  - IPC delta: +1 cycle saved per dynamic JAL ~ +2-3% on CoreMark.

TESTS:

  - test/test_pipeline.py: add a JAL-target test (JAL x0, +12 with
    target instructions at the predicted PC) verifying the target
    retires at order N+1 and pc_rdata=jump_target.
  - test/test_pipeline.py: add a tight JAL self-loop (`JAL x0, -4`)
    verifying steady-state retirement is one-per-cycle (no bubble).
  - test/test_pipeline.py: add a JAL-with-link test (JAL x1, +8)
    verifying x1 receives PC+4 and rd_wdata routes correctly.
  - No changes to test_alu.py, test_decoder.py, test_imm_gen.py, or
    test_reg_file.py: the predictor lives entirely between IF's
    next_pc mux and EX's redirect signal.

changes.description:
Add a static JAL target predictor. Combinational logic derived
from imem_data and the current pc:

  is_jal_w     = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111);
  jal_b1_w     = imem_data[21];
  j_imm_w      = {{11{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31],
                  imem_data[19:12], imem_data[20],
                  imem_data[30:21], 1'b0};
  jal_target_w = pc + j_imm_w;
  predict_jal  = is_jal_w && !(flush || redirect) && !jal_b1_w;

Update the combinational next_pc to a 3:1 priority chain:

  next_pc = redirect    ? redirect_target
          : predict_jal ? jal_target_w
                        : pc + 32'd4;

The PC reg synchronous update keeps its existing
reset/redirect/!stall priority, with next_pc now sourced from
the 3:1 chain. Specifically:

  always_ff @(posedge clock) begin
    if      (reset)    pc <= RESET_PC;
    else if (redirect) pc <= redirect_target;
    else if (!stall)   pc <= next_pc;
  end

Stall STILL holds PC. predict_jal does NOT bypass stall --
this preserves dmem-stall / div_busy / load-use semantics
exactly. When stall clears, predict_jal will still be asserted
(it's combinational on the held imem_data and pc) and PC will
move to jal_target_w on that next cycle. The IF-side invariant
(any JAL captured into ID/EX with valid=1 was predicted) is
preserved across stalls because predict_jal_w stays asserted
while the JAL sits in IF/ID.

The if_id_t output bundle is UNCHANGED:
  out.pc    = pc
  out.instr = (flush || redirect) ? 32'h0000_0013 : imem_data
  out.valid = !(flush || redirect)

Header comment update: latency unchanged (PC reg sync, output
combinational). Document the static JAL predictor and the
IF-side invariant: "PC[1:0] is invariantly 00 in this design
because (a) RESET_PC = 0, (b) sequential update is +4, (c) JAL
prediction targets are computed only when imm[21]=0 and pc[1]
is already 0, (d) JALR/branch redirects with non-zero target[1:0]
are suppressed by misalign_fault in EX. Therefore predict_jal
can gate on imem_data[21] alone without inspecting jal_target_w
bit 1 -- a small Fmax win that keeps the adder result off the
predict gating cone."

DO NOT add any new field to if_id_t or any new pipeline carry
bit. The redirect-suppression in EX is implicit (it inspects
ctrl.is_jump && ctrl.is_jalr only) -- this is the deliberate
design choice that distinguishes this hypothesis from hyp-005
and avoids the formal failure mode.

One-line change to the redirect formula. Today:

  assign redirect = (branch_taken || in.ctrl.is_jump)
                    && !misalign_fault;

New:

  assign redirect = (branch_taken
                      || (in.ctrl.is_jump && in.ctrl.is_jalr))
                    && !misalign_fault;

Effect: JAL (is_jump && !is_jalr) no longer triggers an EX
redirect because IF has already redirected to jump_target via
the static predictor. JALR (is_jump && is_jalr) still
redirects (its target depends on rs1 + imm; IF cannot predict
it without a register read or BTB). Branches still redirect
via branch_taken on the legacy path.

Everything else in ex_stage.sv is UNCHANGED. In particular:
  - branch_cond computation: UNCHANGED.
  - branch_taken expression: UNCHANGED.
  - branch_target adder: UNCHANGED.
  - jalr_sum / jump_target computation: UNCHANGED.
  - redirect_target mux: UNCHANGED. Still
    `in.ctrl.is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target`. (For JAL,
    this branch is still computed combinationally even though
    redirect=0 -- harmless.)
  - misalign_branch / misalign_jump / misalign_fault logic:
    UNCHANGED.
  - ctrl_with_trap mux (the is_illegal/reg_write zero-out on
    trap): UNCHANGED.
  - EX/MEM register's `reg_q.pc_next <= ...`: UNCHANGED. JAL
    retires with pc_next=jump_target as before.
  - EX/MEM register's `reg_q.alu_result <= ...`: UNCHANGED.
    For JAL, reg_q.alu_result still gets in.pc + 32'd4 (link
    register). For JALR, the same path applies.
  - Divider / multiplier handshake: UNTOUCHED.
  - Forwarding muxes (rs1/rs2 selection from EX/MEM and
    MEM/WB): UNTOUCHED.

Add a comment above the new redirect line documenting the
contract: "JAL's redirect is suppressed here because IF's
static predictor has already moved PC to jump_target. The
IF-side invariant (any JAL with valid=1 in ID/EX has had IF
predict to its jump_target) means EX MUST NOT re-redirect for
JAL -- doing so would flush the legitimate target instruction
that's already in IF/ID."

Misaligned JAL handling note: a JAL with imm[21]=1 produces a
misaligned jump_target. The IF predictor's gating
(`!jal_b1_w`) skips prediction for these, so PC stays
sequential. The JAL flows to EX, misalign_jump_w=1,
ctrl_with_trap.is_illegal=1, and redirect=0 (because both
`branch_taken=0` and `is_jalr=0`). pc_next=in.pc+4 per the
misalign override. This is byte-identical to the legacy
misalign-trap path. Verified by hand-trace in the hypothesis
body.

Add three test cases under the existing cocotb runner. None of
them require new RTL signals -- they exercise the JAL predictor
via standard RVFI observation. No changes to test_alu.py /
test_decoder.py / test_imm_gen.py / test_reg_file.py.

1. test_jal_target_prediction
   Program in imem (word addresses):
     0x00: JAL x0, +12      (jumps to 0x0C)
     0x04: ADDI x5, x0, 0xAA  (should NOT retire -- on the wrong
                               path eliminated by IF prediction)
     0x08: ADDI x5, x0, 0xBB  (should NOT retire either)
     0x0C: ADDI x6, x0, 0x42  (the JAL target; SHOULD retire)
   Run for ~10 cycles. Assert:
     - JAL retires with rvfi_pc_rdata=0x00, rvfi_pc_wdata=0x0C.
     - ADDI x6 retires with rvfi_pc_rdata=0x0C and
       rvfi_order = JAL.rvfi_order + 1.
     - Neither ADDI x5 instruction shows up in the rvfi
       retirement stream.
     - x6 = 0x42 after the test.

2. test_tight_jal_loop
   Program: NOP at 0x00, JAL x0, -4 at 0x04 (targets itself).
   Run for ~50 cycles after pipeline fill. Assert:
     - Steady-state rvfi_order increments by ~1 per cycle (NO
       bubble between consecutive JAL retirements).
     - Compare against the pre-prediction baseline expectation
       (one bubble per loop iteration). The new core should be
